input.background: An industrial psychologist conducted an experiment to investigate the relationship between worker productivity and a measure of salary incentive for two manufacturing plants: one with disciplined management practices and the other with a traditional management style. Productivity was measured as the number of machined castings produced by a worker in a 4-week period of 40 hours per week. The incentive was the bonus amount (in cents per casting) paid for all castings produced in excess of 1,000 per worker during the 4-week period. Nine workers were selected from each plant, with three workers assigned to receive a 20¢ bonus, three a 30¢ bonus, and three a 40¢ bonus. Productivity data for the 18 workers are shown in Table 12.4.
input.data:
\begin{tabular}{|l|l|l|l|l|l|l|l|l|l|}
\hline Table 12.4 & \multicolumn{9}{|c|}{Productivity Data for Example 12.11} \\
\hlineManagement style & \multicolumn{9}{|c|}{Incentive} \\
\hline & \multicolumn{3}{|c|}{$20 ¢ /$ casting} & \multicolumn{3}{|c|}{$30 ¢ /$ casting} & \multicolumn{3}{|c|}{$40 ¢ /$ casting} \\
\hline Traditional & 1,435 & 1,512 & 1,491 & 1,583 & 1,529 & 1,610 & 1,601 & 1,574 & 1,636 \\
\hline Disciplined & 1,575 & 1,512 & 1,488 & 1,635 & 1,589 & 1,661 & 1,645 & 1,616 & 1,689 \\
\hline
\end{tabular}
input.question: Do the data provide sufficient evidence to indicate that the rate of increase of worker productivity is different for disciplined and traditional plants? Test at α = 0.10.

output.answer:
If the rate of increase of productivity with incentive (i.e., the slope) for plants with the disciplined management style is different from the corresponding slope for traditional plants, then the interaction β3 will differ from 0. Consequently, we want to test:

H0: β3 = 0
Ha: β3 ≠ 0

Using the t-test, the test statistic and corresponding p-value are:
t = 0.014, p = 0.989

Because α = 0.10 is less than the p-value, we fail to reject H0. There is insufficient evidence to conclude that the traditional and disciplined slopes differ. Thus, the test supports our observation of two nearly identical slopes in part b.
